3A_Bibliographic.citation: Al-shoaibi, A. A. A., Zamora, G., Chu, J., Patel, K. P., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Jackson, D. B., Tapert, S. F., Baker, F. C., & Nagata, J. M. (2024). Family conflict and less parental monitoring were associated with greater screen time in early adolescence. Acta Paediatrica, 113, 2452-2458.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: In this paper, screen time is the OUTCOME, not the exposure. Parental monitoring and family conflict are the exposures.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We excluded participants with missing data for total screen time and confounders (n = 1118), leaving 10757 children for the current analysis.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Parental monitoring score and family conflict score at baseline
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Parental monitoring: youth-reported 5-item validated measure (Likert 1-5). Family conflict: youth-reported conflict subset of the Family Environment Scale (9-item, scale 0-9).
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - exposure is parental monitoring and family conflict, not screen time
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (parental monitoring mean score 1-5; family conflict mean score 0-9)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Parental monitoring was based on validated measures evaluating child perceptions of parental monitoring. At baseline, youth-reported responses used a Likert scale ranging from never (one) to almost always (five). The scale was calculated as the average of the five questions with a range from 1 to 5 with higher scores indicating overall high parental monitoring behaviours. Measures of family conflict were determined from the baseline youth-reported conflict subset of the Family Environment Scale, which was calculated as the average of nine questions evaluating conflict between family members.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is the OUTCOME in this paper, not the exposure.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Total screen time and screen time by modality (TV/movies, YouTube, video games, texting, video chat, social media) at Year 1 and Year 2
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Recreational screen time was obtained from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey where data were collected annually and harmonised across years. Participants answered questions about daily time spent on six different screen modalities... A weighted average was calculated to obtain the participants' average daily screen time.
3D_Outcome.notes: Screen time is the OUTCOME here, not the exposure.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Mixed-effects models with robust standard errors
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race or ethnicity, household income, highest parental education, study site, baseline screen time measures
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used baseline parental monitoring and family conflict scores as primary predictors. Repeated measures of total screen time and screen time separated across the six modalities from Year 1 and Year 2 were used as the outcome... we used mixed-effects models with robust standard errors to assess the association of baseline parental monitoring and family conflict scores with daily screen time measures in Years 1 and 2.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours per day of total screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Parental monitoring Model 2, Total screen time B (95% CI) = -0.37 (-0.58, -0.16)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours per day of total screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Family conflict Model 2, Total screen time B (95% CI) = 0.08 (0.03, 0.12)
Table 2: Parental monitoring Model 2, Video games B (95% CI) = -0.27 (-0.35, -0.19)
Table 2: Parental monitoring Model 2, Videos (YouTube) B (95% CI) = -0.16 (-0.21, -0.10)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: A higher parental monitoring score was associated with less total screen time (B = -0.37, 95% CI -0.58, -0.16), with the strongest associations being with video games and YouTube videos.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The family conflict effect on total screen time (B=0.08) is very small. Parental monitoring effect on total screen time (B=-0.37) is moderate in absolute terms but represents less than half an hour difference across the full parental monitoring scale range.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Our findings reinforce this recommendation and clinicians should actively encourage parents to lead open discussions with their children about appropriate screen time use.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: strategies to reduce screen time in adolescence may target improved communication between parents and their children to strengthen familial relationships
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames parental monitoring as a protective factor against excessive screen time and family conflict as a risk factor. It interprets the findings as supporting clinical recommendations to improve parent-child communication. The paper does not discuss the small magnitude of the family conflict effect (B=0.08 hours/day) and treats all statistically significant findings as clinically meaningful.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is the outcome in this paper, not the exposure. This reverses the typical exposure-outcome relationship in the systematic review. The paper may need special handling in the review.
